concepts.title: Dissolution of Partnership Firm
concepts.description: Dissolution of a partnership firm can be either voluntary or involuntary. Voluntary dissolution occurs when partners agree to dissolve the firm, while involuntary dissolution occurs due to death of a partner, insolvency, etc.

concepts.title: Reasons for Dissolution
concepts.description: Reasons for dissolution include retirement of a partner, death of a partner, insolvency of a partner, admission of a new partner, dissolution by a partner, and court order.

concepts.title: Methods of Accounting for Dissolution
concepts.description: The two methods of accounting for dissolution are: (i) Revaluation method, and (ii) Locking up method. The revaluation method involves revaluing the assets and liabilities of the firm, while the locking up method involves locking up the assets and liabilities of the firm at their book values.

concepts.title: Order of Settlement of Accounts
concepts.description: To remember the order of settlement of accounts, use the mnemonic 'AIPD': (i) Ascertainment of profits or losses, (ii) Intercourse of partners, (iii) Payment of capital and interest, and (iv) Distribution of assets.

concepts.title: Calculation of Amount Due to Partner
concepts.description: Students often forget to include the interest on capital in the calculation of the amount due to a partner. Remember to include interest on capital in the calculation.

concepts.title: Particulars of Account to be Closed
concepts.description: The following accounts are to be closed at the time of dissolution: Partner's Capital A/c, Partner's Current A/c, Profit and Loss A/c, and the Balance Sheet.

concepts.title: Accounting Treatment
concepts.description: The accounting treatment for dissolution involves closing the capital and current accounts of the partners and preparing a statement showing the amount of assets to be distributed among the partners.

concepts.title: Preparation of Statement of Affairs
concepts.description: A statement of affairs is prepared at the time of dissolution, which shows the assets, liabilities, and net amount available for distribution among the partners.
